summary: The reproduction bundle clones Netmaker v1.5.0, copies an EvoHunt helper test into the controllers package, and verifies that an attacker-signed host JWT can delete a foreign host through the fallback host-update route.
expected_security_impact: Running the bundle should show that VerifyHostToken accepts a forged host JWT and that PUT /api/v1/fallback/host/{hostid} with Action=DELETE_HOST removes the target host, printing the EVOHUNT_SUCCESS proof string.
actual_observed_result: Running ./reproduction/run.sh cloned Netmaker commit 6b7d33fa7749c87e6b3c59b1a544d80c727c478b, built the Docker image, mounted the pinned checkout into the container, and the helper test printed EVOHUNT_SUCCESS after a forged host JWT deleted the target host through the fallback update route.
verification_method: The container copies the helper test into the cloned repository, runs go test ./controllers -run TestEvohuntForgedHostJWTDeletesForeignHost -v, and requires the exact EVOHUNT_SUCCESS proof string before reporting success.
evidence_produced_by_run: The containerized helper test printed: EVOHUNT_SUCCESS: forged host JWT deleted foreign host <uuid>.
go test ./controllers -run TestEvohuntForgedHostJWTDeletesForeignHost -v exited successfully inside the container.
The host-side run script printed: [evohunt] SUCCESS: reproduction completed and verified.
limitations_or_blockers: The destructive proof path still needs the target host ID so the request can name the victim host route.

reproduction.source_acquisition: run.sh clones the target repository on the host, checks out the pinned vulnerable commit, and mounts that checkout into the container at /target-repo.
